title: Chapter XLIV.

source.title: NPNF-211. Sulpitius Severus, Vincent of Lerins, John Cassian

verses.book: The Sacred History Of Sulpitius Severus.
verses.text: But these, the fewer they became, showed themselves all the more powerful; as the most steadfast among them was to be reckoned our friend Fœgadius, and Servatio, bishop of the Tungri.

verses.book: The Sacred History Of Sulpitius Severus.
verses.text: As these had not yielded to threats and terrors, Taurus assails them with entreaties, and beseeches them with tears to adopt milder counsels.

verses.book: The Sacred History Of Sulpitius Severus.
verses.text: He argued that the bishops were now in the seventh month since they had been shut up within one city—that no hope of returning home presented itself to them, worn out by the inclemency of winter and positive want; and what then would be the end?

verses.book: The Sacred History Of Sulpitius Severus.
verses.text: He urged them to follow the example of the majority, and to derive authority for so doing at least from the numbers who had preceded them.

verses.book: The Sacred History Of Sulpitius Severus.
verses.text: For Fœgadius openly declared that he was prepared for banishment, and for every kind of punishment that might be assigned him, but would not accept that confession of faith which had been drawn up by the Arians.

verses.book: The Sacred History Of Sulpitius Severus.
verses.text: Thus several days passed in this sort of discussion.

verses.book: The Sacred History Of Sulpitius Severus.
verses.text: And when they made little progress towards a pacification, by degrees Fœgadius began to yield, and at the last was overcome by a proposal which was made to him.

verses.book: The Sacred History Of Sulpitius Severus.
verses.text: For Valens and Ursatius affirmed that the present confession of faith was drawn up on the lines of Catholic doctrine, and having been brought forward by the Easterns at the instigation of the emperor, could not be rejected without impiety; and what possible end of strife could there be if a confession which satisfied the Easterns was rejected by those of the West?

verses.book: The Sacred History Of Sulpitius Severus.
verses.text: Finally, if there appeared anything less fully stated in the present confession than was desirable, they themselves should add what they thought ought to be added, and that they, for their part, would acquiesce in those things which might be added.

verses.book: The Sacred History Of Sulpitius Severus.
verses.text: This friendly profession was received with favorable minds by all.

verses.book: The Sacred History Of Sulpitius Severus.
verses.text: Nor did our people venture any longer to make opposition, desiring as they did in some way or other now to put an end to the business.

verses.book: The Sacred History Of Sulpitius Severus.
verses.text: Then confessions drawn up by Fœgadius and Servatio began to be published; and in these first Arius and his whole unfaithful scheme was condemned, while the Son of God also was pronounced equal to the Father, and without beginning, [that is] without any commencement in time.

verses.book: The Sacred History Of Sulpitius Severus.
verses.text: Then Valens, as if assisting our friends, subjoined the statement (in which there lurked a secret guile) that the Son of God was not a creature like the other creatures; and the deceit involved in this declaration escaped the notice of the hearers.

verses.book: The Sacred History Of Sulpitius Severus.
verses.text: For in these words, in which the Son was denied to be like the other creatures, he was nevertheless pronounced a creature, only superior to the rest.

verses.book: The Sacred History Of Sulpitius Severus.
verses.text: Thus neither party could hold that it had wholly conquered or had wholly been conquered, since the confession itself was in favor of the Arians, but the declarations afterwards added were in favor of our friends.

verses.book: The Sacred History Of Sulpitius Severus.
verses.text: That one, however, must be excepted which Valens had subjoined, and which, not being at the time understood, was at length comprehended when it was too late.

verses.book: The Sacred History Of Sulpitius Severus.
verses.text: In this way, at any rate, the council was brought to an end, a council which had a good beginning but a disgraceful conclusion.
